preamble: The shift from a voiced labial stop [b] to a voiced palatal stop [ɟ] is an instance of 'labial palatalization'. Similar to the shift of [p] to [c], it is usually triggered by a following palatal glide [j] or high front vowel [i]. This process is well-attested in the development of several Bantu languages and in certain southern Italian dialects where labials followed by glides undergo place shifts.

examples.note: Instances where labials shift to palatal or postalveolar articulations under the influence of high front vowels/glides.
Dialectal developments where [bj] shifts to a palatal stop [ɟ].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
